headlines.geopolitics: Iranian Military Shows It Knows How to Adapt, U.S. Officials Say
headlines.economy: Culture at Columbia Shielded Sexual Assault by Physician, Report Finds
headlines.domestic: Fox News Poll: Voters expect AI to transform our lives — but today is not that day

metadata.daily_do: Check one piece of content you share today to see if it's AI-generated. Most browsers now have detection tools built in, or just Google 'AI detector' and paste a link.

clips.caption: When your professor drops historical truth bombs
'Literally Anybody Else' is actually on the ballot
The AI trust problem explained perfectly

script.text: The Soviets had a saying: 'When your enemy adapts, you must adapt faster, or you will find yourself playing yesterday's game with tomorrow's consequences.'
It's Monday, March 10th. I'm Leo Barros, and this is The Friction.

Three headlines this morning. First, geopolitics: U.S. officials say Iran's military is showing tactical adaptability, specifically targeting what they see as American vulnerabilities in our regional air defense systems.
Of course they are.

Second, domestic institutions: An external investigation found Columbia University and NewYork-Presbyterian Hospital mishandled sexual assault allegations against physician Robert Hadden. Two doctors have stepped down following the report.
Jesus. How many more of these are we gonna find?

And third, technology and trust: A new Fox News poll shows 87% of voters expect AI to transform American life, but only 47% find it useful today. Meanwhile, 80% are extremely or very worried about distinguishing real from AI-generated content.
So we're scared of something we also think is useless. That tracks.
Fear and fascination often walk hand in hand.

We'll start with Iran, then dive into what this AI poll tells us about trust in 2026. Plus, Dr. Pringle has thoughts on adaptation in warfare, and Jax found a candidate literally named 'Literally Anybody Else.'
I'm not making that up.

So Iran's military is adapting. The Times reports they're specifically targeting perceived weaknesses in U.S. air defenses protecting our troops and assets in the region. Duke, you served over there. Is this normal military evolution or something more concerning?

It's both, honestly. Look, any competent military studies their opponent and adapts. That's basic doctrine. But the fact that U.S. officials are talking about it publicly means they're either worried enough to send a message, or they want Iran to know we're watching.
Or they're laying groundwork for more military spending. I mean, 'Iran is getting better at fighting us' sounds like a great pitch for upgraded defense systems.
Bree, with all due respect, not everything is a defense contractor conspiracy. Iran has been probing our systems for years. They hit that Saudi facility in 2019, remember? They're good at this.
I'm not saying it's a conspiracy, I'm saying timing matters. We just approved another forty billion for regional defense last month. Now suddenly Iran is super adaptive?
The reporting suggests this adaptation is ongoing, not sudden. What does adaptation look like in practice?

They watch how we respond to attacks. If we shoot down their drones one way, they change the flight pattern next time. If our radar picks up certain signatures, they modify those signatures. It's like— Jenny and I play this game with our kids where we hide their toys, and they get better at finding them every time.
Except in this version, the toys explode.
Right, so the stakes are a little higher.

But here's what I don't get. If they're adapting to our defenses, why aren't we adapting our diplomacy? Like, maybe the reason they're getting better at attacking us is because we keep giving them reasons to want to attack us.
Bree, they've been chanting 'Death to America' for forty years. I don't think our diplomatic tone is the variable here.

And we've been sanctioning them for forty years! Meanwhile, they've gone from basically no nuclear program to almost having a bomb. So clearly the sanctions-plus-military-pressure approach isn't working either.
The reporting doesn't suggest these are offensive preparations. It sounds more like Iran studying how to defend against us if things escalate.
Which is what any military would do. The problem is, in that region, defense and offense look pretty similar. A missile that can take out our air defense can also take out a civilian airport.
True. But maybe that's exactly why we need to be talking to them instead of just... you know, having this endless arms race where both sides keep getting better at killing each other.
Can I just say, as someone who gets anxious when my Wi-Fi goes down, I find it deeply unsettling that there are people whose job is to think about how to blow up our stuff more efficiently?
That has been the human condition for millennia, young man.
Professor, you mentioned Soviet adaptation earlier. Is there a historical parallel here?
Indeed there is. The Cold War was essentially a forty-year seminar in military adaptation. Every move by one side prompted a countermove by the other.

Take the Cuban Missile Crisis. The Soviets placed missiles in Cuba not because they wanted to start a war, but because they had studied American nuclear capabilities and realized they were falling behind. They adapted to what they perceived as an American advantage.
Right, and that almost ended the world.

Precisely. But here's what's instructive: both Kennedy and Khrushchev realized they were trapped in an escalation spiral neither wanted. The resolution came through back-channel diplomacy while maintaining public strength.
But that worked because both sides were rational actors who didn't want nuclear war. I'm not sure Iran's leadership calculates risk the same way.
An interesting point. But consider this: Iran has been fighting proxy wars for decades without directly engaging American forces. That suggests a certain rational restraint, does it not?
Exactly! They're not suicidal. They're strategic. Which is exactly why diplomacy might actually work.

The Soviets also developed increasingly sophisticated ways to test American defenses without triggering war. They would fly bombers toward our airspace and turn back at the last moment, studying our response times and procedures.
That sounds like the geopolitical equivalent of 'I'm not touching you, I'm not touching you.'

That is... actually not an inaccurate analogy. The point is, military adaptation in adversarial relationships follows predictable patterns. Each side studies the other, probes for weaknesses, and develops countermeasures.
Right, but the difference is we had hotlines with Moscow. We don't have that kind of communication infrastructure with Tehran.

Which is precisely why this Iranian adaptation should concern us. Without communication channels, adaptation can look like preparation for attack. Both sides may be responding to perceived threats that don't actually exist.
So the historical lesson is that adaptation without communication leads to misunderstanding?

Among other things, yes. The Soviets had a saying: 'When your enemy adapts, you must adapt faster, or you will find yourself playing yesterday's game with tomorrow's consequences.' But they also understood that adaptation without dialogue is a recipe for miscalculation.
Which brings us back to talking to them instead of just trying to out-adapt them.

I hear you, but talking requires both sides wanting to talk. And right now, Iran's idea of diplomacy seems to be 'remove all sanctions, accept our nuclear program, and oh by the way, death to America.'
All right, let's shift gears. That AI poll has some fascinating numbers about trust and technology. Bree, you've been following this space closely.
Yeah, and these numbers tell a story about where we are right now. 87% think AI will change everything, but only 47% find it useful today. That gap is huge.
It's like everyone's waiting for AI to be amazing while also being terrified that it already is.
But here's the really interesting part: 80% are worried about distinguishing real content from AI-generated content. That's not just about technology, that's about the collapse of shared reality.
I mean, we already had that problem before AI. Half the stuff on social media was fake anyway.
Yeah, but this is different. Before, you needed actual humans to create fake content. Now you can generate a thousand fake news articles in the time it takes me to drink my coffee.
The poll shows only 60% feel confident they can identify AI-generated content. That number hasn't changed since last June.
Which is wild because AI has gotten way better in the last nine months. So either people are getting better at detecting it, or they're just confident in their wrongness.
I bet it's the second one. I showed my mom a deepfake video last week and she was like 'that looks fake.' I showed her a real video and she said the same thing. We're just guessing now.
So what's the solution? Ban AI? Good luck with that. China's not gonna stop developing it because Americans are confused.
No, but we could require disclosure. The poll shows 89% want to be told when content is AI-generated. That's about as close to consensus as you get in America these days.
X just announced they're requiring disclosure for AI-generated videos of armed conflicts. That seems like a start.
Right, but who's gonna enforce that? The AI police? 'Sir, step away from the ChatGPT and show me your disclosure badge.'
Look, the technology exists to watermark AI content automatically. The platforms could build it in. They just don't want to because it might slow down engagement.
And there's the real problem. It's not the technology, it's the business model. Confusion drives clicks.
If I may, this reminds me of the early days of the printing press. People were terrified that anyone could now create convincing pamphlets spreading false information.
Did they figure that one out?
Eventually, yes. Through institutions - journalism standards, fact-checking, legal frameworks. But it took centuries.
Great, so we just need to wait three hundred years and we'll figure out AI. Very reassuring.
Well, we do have one advantage our ancestors didn't: we've seen this pattern before.
All right, time for rapid fire with Jax. What weird news are we not paying attention to?
Oh man, where do I start? First up: Palantir, the AI weapons company, just deployed their artificial intelligence system to help the government find... office chairs.
I'm sorry, what?

Apparently the Pentagon was having trouble tracking furniture inventory, so they hired the company that makes AI to identify terrorist targets to find missing desk chairs. The same algorithm that can spot a weapons cache from satellite imagery is now like, 'Hey, that's a Herman Miller Aeron in conference room B.'
This is either the most American story ever or a sign that we've completely lost the plot.
Why not both? Next story: Louisiana has until Friday to put the Ten Commandments in every classroom, and apparently some districts are just... not gonna do it.
This is the law that passed last year?
Yep. And some school boards are basically going 'come and make us.' It's like constitutional chicken. Who's gonna blink first, the state or the schools?
Nullification crises have a long and complicated history in American federalism.
Right, but usually they're about slavery or taxes, not whether kids need to see 'Thou shalt not covet' next to the periodic table.
I mean, 'thou shalt not steal' isn't terrible life advice for teenagers.
Sure, but do we really need government mandates for basic moral concepts? Also, what about kids who aren't Christian?
Speaking of government, Texas has a mayoral candidate literally named 'Literally Anybody Else.' That's their legal name. They changed it specifically to run for office.
That's... actually brilliant.
Right? Like, imagine being in the voting booth and seeing 'Literally Anybody Else' on the ballot. That's gotta be tempting for about 60% of voters at any given time.
Honestly, depending on the other options, they might get my vote.
This is how democracy dies. Not with a bang, but with a really good joke name.
And finally, Florida man news: a drug dealer locked his customer in a storage unit for months over unpaid debt. The customer just got rescued last week.
How is this rapid fire story and not the lead?
Because it's Florida, so it barely registers anymore. But yeah, apparently the guy owed like eight hundred bucks and got imprisoned in a ten-by-ten storage unit with a bucket and some crackers.
That's kidnapping! That's literally kidnapping!
Yeah, the dealer has been charged with false imprisonment, which seems like undercharging, but hey, Florida.
Debtor's prisons were outlawed in this country in 1833. Apparently no one informed Florida.
To be fair, a lot of laws seem optional in Florida. It's like the Wild West, but with more alligators and worse decision-making.
All right, time for today's Daily Do. With 80% of people worried about distinguishing real from AI-generated content, here's something you can actually control.

Before you share something today - anything, a news article, a funny video, a photo - take thirty seconds to check if it might be AI-generated. Most browsers now have built-in detection tools, or just Google 'AI detector' and paste the link.
And honestly? When in doubt, don't share it. The internet will survive without your questionable repost.
Unless it's really funny. Then all bets are off.
Jax...
I'm kidding! Check first, then laugh. Responsible comedy consumption.
As Benjamin Franklin once said, 'Believe none of what you hear and half of what you see.' In 2026, perhaps we should believe a quarter of what we see and verify the rest.
That's The Friction for Monday, March 10th. I'm Leo Barros.
And remember, if you're ever unsure about what's real anymore, just vote for Literally Anybody Else. At least you'll know what you're getting.
We'll see you tomorrow.
